extra.message: User-supplied 'username' from POST body is interpolated directly into an SQL query string via f-string with no parameterization: cur.execute(f"SELECT * FROM USERS WHERE USERNAME='{username}'"). This allows SQL injection enabling authentication bypass, data exfiltration, or data destruction.

Database exception details are returned directly to the user via return {"msg": f"{e}"}, 200. This exposes internal database error messages (schema details, table names, etc.) to potential attackers, facilitating further attacks.

cwe: CWE-209: Generation of Error Message Containing Sensitive Information

extra.message: User-supplied 'username' from POST body is reflected directly in the JSON response without any sanitization or output encoding: return {"msg": f"Hello, {username}"}, 200. While returned as JSON, if the consuming frontend renders this value into HTML DOM without escaping, it enables reflected Cross-Site Scripting (XSS).

cwe: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation (Cross-Site Scripting)

extra.message: User-supplied 'mathexp' from POST body is concatenated into a Jinja2 Template string and then rendered: test = Template("my temp: " + exp); test.render(). This allows Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI), which can lead to arbitrary Python code execution (RCE) through crafted template expressions such as {{ config }} or {{ self.__init__.__globals__['os'].system('id') }}.

cwe: CWE-94: Improper Control of Generation of Code (Code Injection)

extra.message: User-supplied 'imagelink' URL from POST body is passed directly to requests.get(url=name) with no validation. This enables Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF), allowing an attacker to make the server fetch arbitrary URLs including internal network services (e.g., http://169.254.169.254/ on cloud environments) or file:// URIs. Additionally, TLS certificate verification is disabled with verify=False.

TLS certificate verification is explicitly disabled with verify=False in requests.get(). This makes the application vulnerable to man-in-the-middle (MITM) attacks, as the server will accept any TLS certificate including self-signed or malicious ones when making outbound HTTPS requests.

The Host header value from the incoming request is reflected directly into an HTML anchor tag in the response: return {"msg": f"response, <a href='{h}'>"}, 200. An attacker can inject a malicious Host header (e.g., Host: evil.com) to craft a phishing/reset-password link that appears to come from the legitimate application, enabling open redirect and phishing attacks.

cwe: CWE-601: URL Redirection to Untrusted Site (Open Redirect)

extra.message: User-supplied 'filename' from POST body is passed directly to open().read() with no path sanitization: file = open(name).read(). This allows path traversal (Local File Inclusion), enabling an attacker to read arbitrary files on the system by supplying paths like ../../../etc/passwd or /proc/self/environ.

cwe: CWE-22: Improper Limitation of a Pathname to a Restricted Directory (Path Traversal)

extra.message: Flask application is run with debug=True which enables the Werkzeug interactive debugger. If exposed in production, this allows remote code execution via the interactive debugger console and exposes stack traces with sensitive information to users. Additionally, the server binds to 0.0.0.0 which exposes it on all network interfaces.

Hardcoded user credentials are seeded into the database at application startup: 'mike'/'kaines' (line 25) and 'admin'/'admin' (line 28). These credentials are embedded in source code, making them visible to anyone with access to the repository. The admin/admin credential in particular provides privileged access with a well-known default password.
